title: Deaths from volcanic eruptions
subtitle: Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.
caption:
Deaths from volcanic eruptions

Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.

This bar chart displays the number of deaths from volcanic eruptions worldwide from 1570 to 2024. The vertical axis measures deaths from 0 to 60,000, with grid lines marking intervals of 10,000. The horizontal axis represents the timeline, with specific years labeled at irregular intervals. The data is characterized by long periods of low mortality punctuated by several extreme spikes. The highest visible bar occurs around 1815, reaching the 60,000 mark. Two other prominent peaks stand out: one around 1902, reaching just above 40,000, and another around 1883, extending past 30,000. A fourth significant spike appears near 1985, with a value between 20,000 and 30,000. A smaller cluster of bars in the late 1700s shows values reaching approximately 15,000 and 10,000. The year 1570 shows a bar reaching 10,000. Throughout the rest of the timeline, particularly between 1600 and 1750 and from 1950 to 2024, the bars remain very low, mostly staying below the 5,000 level. The chart includes direct deaths alongside secondary impacts like tsunamis or earthquakes triggered by eruptions, and notes that recent data is likely more complete than older historical estimates.
